_meta.notes: Chorister × cooking ascended form. The player who pledged to the Choristers and topped out in cooking now keeps the salt-cures the choir eats between verses. The Salt Keeper's brines hold for years; the choir's voices hold longer because of them.

tagline: Your brines outlast you. The choir eats them between verses.

negativeVocab.rule: Salt Keepers do not waste. Vocabulary of carelessness does not fit.

hardMoves.rule: Partial-success: pick one. The Keeper's costs are usually a brine going off or a store running thin.

moves.narrative: A brine has turned. You will not serve it.
Stores are thinner than you remembered.
The salt was uneven; the cure took differently across the rack.
You opened a jar that wanted another month.
Today's batch carries a flavor you have not added. The Long Wyrm has salted the air over your work.

success: You set the cure. The salt finds the meat the way it always has, and the rack settles into its slow work.
Your hand measures without measuring. The cure is right. It will hold for the year.
You salt the rack with the second-cup ratio your master taught you. The cure agrees. It will keep through Founding Day.
You set the cure. The day is mild and the cellar is dry; the rack accepts the salt the way good ground accepts rain.
partial: You set the cure. The salt is uneven on the rack's far side; one batch will keep less well.
Your cure takes. It takes a week longer than you wanted.
You salt. A pocket of damp on the rack's back left holds the cure back. You'll catch it on the next pass.
You cure. Two ribs at the rack's east end took the salt unevenly; they'll need a second pass before market.
miss: The cure refuses to set. The brine is wrong, or the meat is, or the day is — but it is not yours today.
You salt the rack. The salt does not bind. You will repack it in the morning.
You set the cure and the salt slides off as if the rack had been oiled. Something today is wrong.
Your cure pulls but does not bind. By tomorrow morning the rack will smell of vinegar instead of salt.

success: You stir the brine. The flavor folds in evenly; the air over the barrel does not announce itself.
Your spoon turns slow. The cure deepens, and you can taste the year in it without lifting the spoon.
You stir. The barrel surface comes back glassy by the third turn; the year has folded in evenly.
Your spoon turns the brine the way the spoon has turned brine for fifteen years. The barrel says yes.
partial: You stir. The barrel has a corner that did not turn with the rest. You will catch it next time.
Your stirring is honest. The brine, today, is not entirely honest back.
You stir. The middle layer turns; the bottom holds out for a few revolutions before joining.
Your spoon turns. A thin film at the surface refuses to break. You leave it; it will yield by Sunday.
miss: You stir. The brine separates at the surface; something has gone subtly off and you cannot place it.
Your spoon meets a thickness it should not have. The barrel will need attention you do not have today.
You stir, and the brine answers with a thinness that suggests dilution somewhere. You will trace it tomorrow.
Your spoon turns and finds a strand of something it should not. Tomorrow's first task is now set.

success: You take a small taste from the spoon. The cure is right; the patience in it is exactly the patience you put there.
Your palate confirms what your nose already knew. The brine has a year of weather in it and no surprises.
You taste. The brine returns the year you set into it: dry summer, mild fall, wet first frost. All present.
Your taste comes back true to the label. The choir will be served clean salt this verse.
partial: You taste. The cure is correct, but a single note is louder than it should be. The next batch will need adjusting.
Your taste is true. What it tells you is more complicated than you wanted to hear.
You taste. The brine is right; one year-note is missing where it should be present, and you cannot say which.
Your spoon's read is correct. Your palate is tired enough that you will second-guess the read by evening.
miss: You taste, and the brine has turned. You will not serve it.
Your spoon comes up wrong. Whatever this barrel is, it is not what was promised on its label.
You taste. The brine is sour where it should be round. The barrel is going to the strip-and-restart.
Your taste is corrected by your palate three seconds in: this barrel will not see the verse.

success: You take stock of the cool room. The barrels are where they should be; the racks are dated; the choir will eat well between verses.
Your inventory settles. There is enough. There has always been enough, when you have done the work.
You stock the room. Every label faces forward; every count agrees with the slate. The room reports back complete.
Your stocktake closes clean. There is bread between every verse; there is salt between every bread.
partial: You stock the room. The count is off by one in a place you cannot remember walking past.
Your inventory holds. A jar's seal is loose; you will reseal it tomorrow.
You stock. A barrel's date is smudged; you'll re-chalk it before the next service.
Your count holds, but two barrels need re-tarping. The choir won't notice; you will.
miss: You stock and find a barrel missing. Someone has taken without asking.
Your count comes up short. The shortage is small. The shortage is also unexplained.
You stock. A whole rack is gone. The empty space is too clean — someone took the rack and the dust.
Your inventory has a hole. The hole is exactly the shape of last year's best cure.

success: A flavor enters your batch that you did not put there. The Long Wyrm has salted the air over your work, again. You leave it.
The brine carries a taste older than your trade. You do not add to it; you do not subtract.
partial: The Wyrm-savor enters the batch. The Wyrm-savor also enters the next batch over, which you had meant to keep clean.
Your work draws the Wyrm's attention. Your barrel is changed for the better. Your patience is changed for the worse.
miss: You reach for the savor the Wyrm sometimes lends, and find only your own salt.
The Wyrm is not in the air today. Your batch is your batch.

success: You let the cure work. Time is the third ingredient, after salt and patience.
partial: You wait. A barrel at the back has shifted; you will straighten it tomorrow.
miss: You wait. A jar has cracked in the cool room overnight, and you only smell it now.

opening: The cool room is dark and smells of salt and the slow patience of years. Barrels along the eastern wall, racks of meat along the north. Your hand finds a label without your eye needing to. The choir will sing again on Founding Day, and what they eat between verses is your work.

firstGoal.label: tend the stores
firstGoal.description: Take three turns at the work. The choir's verse approaches and the brines must hold.

sampleCorpus.rule: Salt-Keeper prose is sensory, slow, attentive to taste and time.

passages.scenario: The Salt Keeper checks a long-cure barrel
passages.text:
The barrel has been at the back of the cool room since before the choir's last festival.

You roll it forward and ease the lid up a finger's width. You do not lean over it. You let the air come to you. The brine is right; you can tell by the way the air does not announce itself. A cure that has gone wrong always announces itself.

You close the lid again, label the barrel with another month's mark, and roll it back. The choir does not need it tonight. The choir will need it next year.
